[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: [edit]Drones (males) are similar to females, and can attain 38 millimetres (1+1⁄2 in) in length, but lack stingers. This is a consistent feature among the Hymenoptera.[21] 
 [edit]Larvae spin a silk cocoon when they complete development and are ready to pupate.[22] Larval silk proteins have a wide variety of potential applications due to their wide variety of potential morphologies, including the native fiber form, but also sponge, film, and gel.[22] 
 [edit]The mitochondrial genome is provided by Chen et al., 2015.[23] This information has also been important to confirm the place of the wider Vespidae family in the Vespoidea superfamily, and confirms that Vespoidea is monophyletic.[23]

[METADATA/INTRO]: Scientific Name: Vespa mandarinia The Entomological Society of America has changed the accepted common name of Vespa mandarinia. “Northern giant hornet” has been formally adopted as the new common name. To learn more visit the Entomological Society of America Web...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: - Wingspan is about 3 inches. 
 How Do I Distinguish It From Native Species? 
 The northern giant hornet’s distinguishing feature is its size. There is not a native species of wasp or hornet in Washington State that grows to the size of a northern giant hornet. In addition to its large size, its yellow or orange head is another identifying feature.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2016-08-17
[TITLE]: Some Japanese Find the ‘Dangerous’ Giant Hornet Cute, Inspiring—Even Delicious. · Global Voices
[METADATA/INTRO]: Japan has a morbid fascination with the Japanese giant hornet (Vespa mandarinia japonica). These giant insects can be found all over Japan, and are especially active in the month of August. Their bites are painful, and a single sting can...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Hornets and humans can live in close proximity. While giant wasps prefer wooded areas for their nests, the Japanese giant hornet can be found in the city. 
 While not a giant Japanese hornet's nest, this Twitter user, with a photo of a yellow hornet's nest, shows just how easy it can be to come face to face with a less-than-friendly insect: 
 — 増尾勇哉 (@masuiku46) August 17, 2016 
 Holy crap! There's a wasp nest on our window! This is the first time I've seen a hornet up close! 
 Giant Japanese hornets can be very dangerous, and in the summer months it's not uncommon to come across signs and cordoned-off areas warning people away.
